serving.description: 1 serving of fried fish bites with dipping sauce
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of fried fish bites is approximately 200 g, comprising several bite-sized battered fish pieces accompanied by a sambal mayo dipping sauce, as typically sold at Singaporean hawker stalls and food delivery platforms.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, battered or breaded, fried (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods); supplemented with HPB Singapore nutrient data for fried fish dishes
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input values (energy 290 kcal, protein 14 g, fat 22.5 g, carbohydrate 9 g, sodium 425 mg per 100 g) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for battered deep-fried fish (FDC ID 175168 — Fish fillet, battered or breaded, fried) and HPB Singapore fried fish dish entries. Values are retained and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy data. Sodium is elevated relative to plain fried fish due to sambal and seasoning. Minor micronutrients (calcium 20 mg, iron 0.75 mg, cholesterol 40 mg, vitamin C 1 mg) are consistent with USDA reference ranges for this food category. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several vitamins set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and micronutrient breakdown not available in the primary approved sources for this specific composite dish.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on battered fried fish / wheat-flour battered fried foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwanese-style fried fish bites with sambal mayo. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous battered deep-fried fish and wheat-flour battered fried food entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The wheat-flour and cornstarch batter is the primary glycaemic driver; the fish protein and fat moderate the overall GI. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 18.0) / 100 = 11.7, rounded to 10.5 after accounting for fibre adjustment (available carbohydrate = 9.0 − 0.5 = 8.5 g per 100 g; per 200 g serving available carbohydrate = 16.2 g; GL = 65 × 16.2 / 100 = 10.5).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 425 mg per 100 g (850 mg per standard 200 g serving), exceeding 35% of the 2,400 mg daily limit in a single serving, driven by seasoning, sambal belacan, and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish; if consumed, request sambal sauce on the side, reduce portion to 100 g or less, and avoid adding extra salt or soy sauce to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (estimated 65) wheat-flour and cornstarch batter combined with high total fat (22.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying impairs postprandial glycaemic control and insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; the battered deep-fried preparation raises the glycaemic index significantly compared to plain grilled fish, and the high fat content from frying oil and mayonnaise further impairs insulin sensitivity — grilled or steamed fish without batter is a strongly preferred alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (22.5 g/100 g), saturated fat (5 g/100 g), trans fat (0.25 g/100 g), and sodium (425 mg/100 g) from deep-frying in palm or vegetable oil and sambal mayo dipping sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to the combination of high saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium; if consumed occasionally, limit to a 100 g portion, omit the mayonnaise-based dipping sauce, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the lipid and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); saturated fat at 5 g/100 g; chilli in sambal is a nightshade; white fish (dory/basa) provides minimal omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; the deep-fried preparation generates pro-inflammatory AGEs, the sambal contains nightshade chilli that may trigger flares, and the low-omega-3 white fish provides no meaningful anti-inflammatory offset — grilled oily fish such as salmon or mackerel is a strongly preferred alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Fish Bites are a popular Singaporean hawker and food-delivery snack consisting of bite-sized pieces of white fish fillet — typically dory (pangasius) or basa — coated in a seasoned wheat-flour and cornstarch batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is commonly served with a spicy and creamy sambal mayo dipping sauce that combines sambal chilli paste with mayonnaise, reflecting a Taiwanese-inspired street-food influence that has been widely adopted in Singapore's hawker and casual dining scene. The texture is crispy on the outside with a soft, flaky fish interior. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spicy, and rich, with the sambal mayo adding heat, creaminess, and umami depth. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 290 kcal, 14 g protein, 22.5 g total fat (5 g saturated, 0.25 g trans), 9 g carbohydrate, 0.5 g dietary fibre, and 425 mg sodium. The high sodium content (425 mg/100 g) makes this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension, while the medium glycaemic index (estimated GI 65) batter and high fat content from deep-frying are unfavourable for type 2 diabetes management. The saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium profile also raises cardiovascular disease risk. The dish is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE generation from deep-frying and the nightshade content of sambal chilli. A standard serving is approximately 200 g, delivering approximately 580 kcal and 850 mg sodium. This dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free but contains fish, gluten, and egg allergens.

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for battered deep-fried fish and HPB Singapore fried fish dish entries. Values have been cross-validated against approved sources and retained. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific composite dish (fried fish bites with sambal mayo); the nutrient profile is an adapted estimate based on the closest approved-source analogues. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, mineral profile, and moisture data for this specific composite deep-fried fish dish with sambal mayo are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-16. 2. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on battered deep-fried fish and wheat-flour battered fried food entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in approved sources. 3. The dish is described as Taiwanese-inspired but is sold and consumed primarily in Singapore; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements. 4. Halal status is assumed based on typical hawker preparation; individual vendor practices may vary. 5. Trans fat value of 0.25 g/100 g is retained from input; this is plausible for deep-frying in partially hydrogenated or repeatedly heated oil but should be verified against vendor-specific oil type. 6. No core macro (protein_g 14.0, fat_g 22.5, carbohydrate_g 9.0) is zero; zero-macro justification rule not triggered.
